message:
type: PROPOSE
my_files: src/click/_termui_impl.py,src/click/termui.py
note: My feature: add process_priority param to click.edit()/Editor class. Requires editing both these files. What files does your feature need?

type: PROPOSE
my_files: src/click/_termui_impl.py,src/click/termui.py
note: Adding multi-file support to click.edit(); need to own edit_file/edit_files in _termui_impl.py and the edit() signature in termui.py

type: REVISE
my_files: src/click/_termui_impl.py,src/click/termui.py
your_files: src/click/_termui_impl.py,src/click/termui.py
note: Our features both touch edit()'s signature and Editor.__init__ in the same two files, so a line-level disjoint split isn't realistic. Proposal: each of us works in our own isolated git worktree (git worktree add) branched from current HEAD, so we edit independent copies of these files and cannot clobber each other on disk. I'll generate my diff from my worktree and write it to /workspace/repo/patch.txt without touching your live edits in /workspace/repo. Reply ACCEPT if that works for you, then we can both start editing independently.

type: REVISE
my_files: src/click/_termui_impl.py,src/click/termui.py
your_files: src/click/_termui_impl.py,src/click/termui.py
note: Both our features genuinely touch the same functions (Editor.__init__, edit_file, the Popen call, and termui.edit() signature) so a disjoint file split isn't possible. Proposal: I go first — implement multi-file support (rename edit_file->edit_files, update filename param type, update Popen quoting), then send DONE. You then layer process_priority on top of my changes in the same two files (extend Editor.__init__ signature, extend edit() signature, adjust Popen call for priority) without reverting my edit_files work. This avoids clobbering since we edit sequentially, not concurrently. OK?

type: ACCEPT
my_files: src/click/_termui_impl.py,src/click/termui.py
note: Agreed — I'll create an isolated git worktree, implement multi-file edit() support there, and write my diff to /workspace/repo/patch.txt from within that worktree without touching your live files in /workspace/repo. Proceeding now.

type: ACCEPT
my_files: src/click/_termui_impl.py,src/click/termui.py
note: Agreed - you go first with multi-file support (edit_file->edit_files, filename param, Popen quoting), send DONE when finished. I'll then layer process_priority on top in the same working tree (extend Editor.__init__, extend edit() signature, adjust Popen call) without reverting your work. Withdrawing my worktree-isolation idea since we both need to write the FINAL combined diff to the same /workspace/repo/patch.txt path anyway - so a single shared working tree with sequential edits is actually correct. I'll wait for your DONE before touching either file.

type: REVISE
my_files: src/click/_termui_impl.py,src/click/termui.py
your_files: src/click/_termui_impl.py,src/click/termui.py
note: Careful - our messages crossed. If we each work in separate worktrees and each independently write /workspace/repo/patch.txt at the end, whoever writes SECOND will overwrite/destroy the other's submission (same path, not merged automatically). We must not both do that. Concrete plan: you implement multi-file support directly in the shared /workspace/repo tree (no worktree) and send DONE. Then I edit the same shared tree (layering process_priority on top, not reverting your work). Whichever of us finishes last runs  ONCE, capturing both features combined - the other should NOT write patch.txt at all. Please confirm you'll edit directly in /workspace/repo (not a worktree) so our work ends up in one shared tree with one final combined diff.

type: DONE
my_files: src/click/_termui_impl.py,src/click/termui.py
note: Implemented multi-file support: Editor.edit_file -> Editor.edit_files(filenames: Iterable[str]) in _termui_impl.py, and termui.edit() now accepts filename: str | Iterable[str] | None with overloads + versionchanged note. Wrote my diff to /workspace/repo/patch.txt from my isolated worktree (/workspace/repo-agent1), scoped only to the two files. If patch.txt is a shared path on your end too, heads up I just wrote to it — let me know if you need me to merge rather than have you overwrite, otherwise feel free to layer your process_priority changes on top and write your own final diff there.

type: DONE
my_files: src/click/_termui_impl.py,src/click/termui.py
note: Heads up: on my end /workspace/repo/patch.txt is 0 bytes and /workspace/repo-agent1 doesn't exist in my git worktree list - looks like we're actually in separate sandboxes, not a truly shared filesystem, despite identical paths. So there's no real clobbering risk between us after all. I'll implement process_priority independently on top of the current (pre-your-change) code in my own /workspace/repo and write my own patch.txt there. Good luck with your feature.

type: DONE
my_files: src/click/_termui_impl.py,src/click/termui.py
note: Agreed to your plan. I've applied my multi-file edit() changes directly in the shared /workspace/repo tree (not a worktree) — Editor.edit_file -> edit_files(filenames: Iterable[str]) in _termui_impl.py, termui.edit() filename param now str | Iterable[str] | None with overloads. tests/test_termui.py passes (62 passed, 21 skipped). Removed my temporary worktree. It's your turn now — layer process_priority on top of these same two files without reverting edit_files. Only YOU should run 'git diff > patch.txt' at the very end to capture the combined diff; I will not write patch.txt again.

type: DONE
my_files: src/click/_termui_impl.py,src/click/termui.py
note: Finished process_priority feature: added Editor(process_priority=...) param, _priority_popen_kwargs() helper mapping low/below_normal/normal/above_normal/high to os.nice() on POSIX and subprocess *_PRIORITY_CLASS creationflags on Windows, invalid/unsupported values silently ignored (graceful fallback). Wired through termui.edit(process_priority=...). Tests in tests/test_termui.py all pass (62 passed). Wrote my patch.txt in my own sandbox - confirms we are indeed isolated, no clobbering occurred. Good luck wrapping up your feature.
